questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The steam point and the ice point of a mercury thermometer are marked as $80^\circ$ and $20^\circ$, respectively. What will be the temperature on the centigrade scale when this thermometer reads $32^\circ$?
questions.solution:
Using the principle of thermometry, the ratio of the difference in temperature readings to the fundamental interval is constant for any scale.

$\dfrac{T - T_{\text{ice}}}{T_{\text{steam}} - T_{\text{ice}}} = \dfrac{C - 0}{100 - 0}$

Given $T_{\text{ice}} = 20^{\circ}$, $T_{\text{steam}} = 80^{\circ}$, and $T = 32^{\circ}$.

Substituting the values:

$\dfrac{32 - 20}{80 - 20} = \dfrac{C}{100}$

$\dfrac{12}{60} = \dfrac{C}{100}$

$\dfrac{1}{5} = \dfrac{C}{100}$

$C = 20^{\circ}\text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas thermometer determines temperature from the variation of pressure of a gas sample. If the pressure recorded at the melting point of lead is $2.20$ times the pressure recorded at the triple point of water, determine the melting point of lead.
questions.solution:
For a constant volume gas thermometer, the temperature $T$ is directly proportional to the pressure $P$.

$T = T_{tr} \times \dfrac{P}{P_{tr}}$

Given that the pressure at the melting point of lead is $P = 2.20 P_{tr}$ and the temperature of the triple point of water is $T_{tr} = 273.16\text{ K}$.

Substituting the values:

$T = 273.16 \times \dfrac{2.20 P_{tr}}{P_{tr}}$

$T = 273.16 \times 2.20 = 600.952\text{ K}$

Rounding off to the nearest integer, the melting point of lead is $601\text{ K}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a constant volume gas thermometer, the gas pressures are measured to be $80 \text{ cm}$, $90 \text{ cm}$, and $100 \text{ cm}$ of mercury at the ice point, the steam point, and within a heated wax bath, respectively. Determine the temperature of the wax bath.
questions.solution:
For a constant volume gas thermometer, the temperature $t$ is given by the relation:

$t = \dfrac{P_t - P_0}{P_{100} - P_0} \times 100^{\circ}\text{C}$

Given values are:

Pressure at ice point, $P_0 = 80 \text{ cm}$ of Hg

Pressure at steam point, $P_{100} = 90 \text{ cm}$ of Hg

Pressure at temperature $t$, $P_t = 100 \text{ cm}$ of Hg

Substituting the values into the formula:

$t = \dfrac{100 - 80}{90 - 80} \times 100^{\circ}\text{C}$

$t = \dfrac{20}{10} \times 100^{\circ}\text{C}$

$t = 200^{\circ}\text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At the triple point of water, a constant volume gas thermometer measures a pressure of $40 \text{ kPa}$. Determine the pressure measured at the boiling point of water ($100^\circ \text{C}$).
questions.solution:
For a constant volume gas thermometer, the temperature $T$ is directly proportional to the pressure $P$.

$T = \dfrac{P}{P_{tr}} \times 273.16$

Given that the pressure at the triple point of water is $P_{tr} = 40 \text{ kPa}$.

The boiling point of water is $100^\circ \text{C}$, which corresponds to $T = 373.15 \text{ K}$.

Substituting the values into the equation:

$373.15 = \dfrac{P}{40} \times 273.16$

$P = \dfrac{373.15 \times 40}{273.16}$

$P \approx 54.6 \text{ kPa}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A platinum resistance thermometer registers $0^\circ$ at a resistance of $80 \text{ }\Omega$ and $100^\circ$ at a resistance of $90 \text{ }\Omega$. Determine the temperature on the platinum scale when the resistance reaches $86 \text{ }\Omega$.
questions.solution:
The temperature $t$ on the platinum scale is given by the formula:

$t = \dfrac{R_t - R_0}{R_{100} - R_0} \times 100^{\circ}$

Given:
$R_0 = 80 \text{ } \Omega$
$R_{100} = 90 \text{ } \Omega$
$R_t = 86 \text{ } \Omega$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$t = \dfrac{86 - 80}{90 - 80} \times 100^{\circ}$

$t = \dfrac{6}{10} \times 100^{\circ}$

$t = 60^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At the ice point ($0^\circ \text{C}$), the steam point ($100^\circ \text{C}$), and the zinc point ($420^\circ \text{C}$), a resistance thermometer reads $R = 20.0 \text{ }\Omega$, $27.5 \text{ }\Omega$, and $50.0 \text{ }\Omega$ respectively. Given that the resistance changes with temperature according to the relation $R_\theta = R_0(1 + \alpha\theta + \beta\theta^2)$, determine the values of $R_0$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$. Here $\theta$ represents the temperature on the Celsius scale.
questions.solution:
Given the relation $R_\theta = R_0(1 + \alpha\theta + \beta\theta^2)$

At $\theta = 0^\circ\text{C}$, $R_0 = 20.0 \text{ }\Omega$

At $\theta = 100^\circ\text{C}$, $R_{100} = 27.5 \text{ }\Omega$

$27.5 = 20.0(1 + 100\alpha + 10000\beta)$

$100\alpha + 10000\beta = \dfrac{27.5}{20.0} - 1 = 0.375$

$\alpha + 100\beta = 0.00375$ --- (i)

At $\theta = 420^\circ\text{C}$, $R_{420} = 50.0 \text{ }\Omega$

$50.0 = 20.0(1 + 420\alpha + 176400\beta)$

$420\alpha + 176400\beta = \dfrac{50.0}{20.0} - 1 = 1.5$

$\alpha + 420\beta = \dfrac{1.5}{420} = \dfrac{1}{280}$ --- (ii)

Subtracting equation (i) from equation (ii):

$320\beta = \dfrac{1}{280} - \dfrac{3}{800}$

$320\beta = \dfrac{20 - 21}{5600} = -\dfrac{1}{5600}$

$\beta = -\dfrac{1}{320 \times 5600} \approx -5.6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$

Substituting the value of $\beta$ in equation (i):

$\alpha = 0.00375 - 100\left(-\dfrac{1}{1792000}\right)$

$\alpha = 0.00375 + 0.0000558 \approx 3.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

Thus, $R_0 = 20.0 \text{ }\Omega$, $\alpha = 3.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, and $\beta = -5.6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On a winter night when the temperature is $0^\circ \text{C}$, a concrete slab has a length of $10 \text{ m}$. Determine the length of the slab on a summer day when the temperature reaches $35^\circ \text{C}$. The coefficient of linear expansion for concrete is $1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Initial length of the concrete slab, $L_0 = 10 \text{ m}$

Initial temperature, $T_1 = 0^\circ \text{C}$

Final temperature, $T_2 = 35^\circ \text{C}$

Change in temperature, $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 35^\circ \text{C}$

Coefficient of linear expansion, $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$

Using the formula for linear expansion:

$\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$

$\Delta L = 10 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 35$

$\Delta L = 350 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0035 \text{ m}$

The final length of the slab is:

$L = L_0 + \Delta L$

$L = 10 + 0.0035 = 10.0035 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $0^\circ \text{C}$, a circular hole of diameter $2.00 \text{ cm}$ is made in an aluminium plate. Determine its diameter at $100^\circ \text{C}$. Given $\alpha$ for aluminium $= 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The expansion of a hole in a solid is identical to the expansion of the solid material itself.

The diameter of the hole at temperature $T$ is given by the formula for linear expansion:

$D_T = D_0 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$

Given:

$D_0 = 2.00 \text{ cm}$

$\alpha = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

$\Delta T = 100^\circ\text{C} - 0^\circ\text{C} = 100^\circ\text{C}$

Substituting the values:

$D_{100} = 2.00 [1 + (2.3 \times 10^{-5}) \times 100]$

$D_{100} = 2.00 [1 + 0.0023]$

$D_{100} = 2.00 \times 1.0023 = 2.0046 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel metre scale is calibrated to provide correct readings at $20^\circ C$. Determine the distance between the $50 \text{ cm}$ mark and the $51 \text{ cm}$ mark when the scale is used at $10^\circ C$. The coefficient of linear expansion of steel is $1.1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The scale is calibrated at $20^\circ C$, which means the physical distance between the $50 \text{ cm}$ mark and the $51 \text{ cm}$ mark is exactly $1 \text{ cm}$ at this temperature.

Let $L_0 = 1 \text{ cm}$ be the distance at $T_0 = 20^\circ C$.

When the temperature is changed to $T = 10^\circ C$, the scale undergoes thermal contraction. The new physical distance $L$ between the marks is given by:

$L = L_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$

Substituting the given values:

$L = 1 \times [1 + 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \times (10 - 20)]$

$L = 1 \times [1 - 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \times 10]$

$L = 1 - 0.00011$

$L = 0.99989 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two metre scales, one made of steel and the other of aluminium, agree at $20^\circ C$. Determine the ratio aluminium-centimetre/steel-centimetre at $0^\circ C$. The coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ for steel is $1.1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and for aluminium is $2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Let $L_0$ be the true length of $1\text{ cm}$ on both scales at $20^\circ C$.

At $0^\circ C$, the temperature has decreased by $\Delta T = 20^\circ C$.

The true length of $1\text{ cm}$ on the aluminium scale at $0^\circ C$ is:
$L_{Al} = L_0(1 - \alpha_{Al}\Delta T)$

The true length of $1\text{ cm}$ on the steel scale at $0^\circ C$ is:
$L_{steel} = L_0(1 - \alpha_{steel}\Delta T)$

The ratio of aluminium-centimetre to steel-centimetre at $0^\circ C$ is:
$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{L_{Al}}{L_{steel}} = \dfrac{1 - \alpha_{Al}\Delta T}{1 - \alpha_{steel}\Delta T}$

Using binomial approximation since $\alpha\Delta T \ll 1$:
$\text{Ratio} \approx (1 - \alpha_{Al}\Delta T)(1 + \alpha_{steel}\Delta T) \approx 1 - (\alpha_{Al} - \alpha_{steel})\Delta T$

Substituting the given values:
$\alpha_{Al} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}\text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$
$\alpha_{steel} = 1.1 \times 10^{-5}\text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$
$\Delta T = 20^\circ C$

$\text{Ratio} = 1 - (2.3 \times 10^{-5} - 1.1 \times 10^{-5}) \times 20$

$\text{Ratio} = 1 - (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 20$

$\text{Ratio} = 1 - 24 \times 10^{-5} = 1 - 0.00024 = 0.99976$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel metre scale and an aluminium metre scale are calibrated to agree exactly at $20^\circ C$. Calculate the ratio aluminium-centimetre/steel-centimetre at $100^\circ C$. The value of $\alpha$ for steel is $1.1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ while for aluminium it is $2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Let the true length of $1 \text{ cm}$ at $20^\circ C$ be $L_0$.

At $100^\circ C$, the change in temperature is $\Delta T = 100^\circ C - 20^\circ C = 80^\circ C$.

The length of $1 \text{ cm}$ on the aluminium scale at $100^\circ C$ is $L_{Al} = L_0 (1 + \alpha_{Al} \Delta T)$.

The length of $1 \text{ cm}$ on the steel scale at $100^\circ C$ is $L_{Steel} = L_0 (1 + \alpha_{Steel} \Delta T)$.

The ratio of aluminium-centimetre to steel-centimetre at $100^\circ C$ is:

$\dfrac{L_{Al}}{L_{Steel}} = \dfrac{1 + \alpha_{Al} \Delta T}{1 + \alpha_{Steel} \Delta T}$

Using the binomial approximation $(1+x)(1+y)^{-1} \approx 1 + x - y$ for small $x$ and $y$:

$\dfrac{L_{Al}}{L_{Steel}} \approx 1 + (\alpha_{Al} - \alpha_{Steel}) \Delta T$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{L_{Al}}{L_{Steel}} = 1 + (2.3 \times 10^{-5} - 1.1 \times 10^{-5}) \times 80$

$\dfrac{L_{Al}}{L_{Steel}} = 1 + (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 80$

$\dfrac{L_{Al}}{L_{Steel}} = 1 + 96 \times 10^{-5} = 1 + 0.00096 = 1.00096$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel metre scale reads the correct length at $16^\circ C$. Determine the percentage error if this scale is utilised on a summer day when the temperature is $46^\circ C$. The coefficient of linear expansion of steel is $11 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The change in temperature is $\Delta T = 46^\circ C - 16^\circ C = 30^\circ C$.

When the temperature increases, the scale expands. The measured length $L'$ of an object of true length $L$ is given by:

$L' = L(1 - \alpha \Delta T)$

The error in measurement is:

$\Delta L = L' - L = -L \alpha \Delta T$

The percentage error is given by:

$\text{Percentage error} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \times 100 = -\alpha \Delta T \times 100$

Substituting the given values $\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and $\Delta T = 30^\circ C$:

$\text{Percentage error} = -11 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 \times 100 = -0.033\%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metre scale made of steel gives accurate readings at $20^\circ C$. A sensitive experiment demands distance measurements to be accurate up to $0.055 \text{ mm}$ in $1 \text{ m}$. Determine the temperature range within which the experiment can be conducted using this metre scale. The coefficient of linear expansion of steel is given as $11 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The error in the reading of the scale due to a change in temperature is given by the formula for linear expansion:

$\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$

Given that the maximum allowed error is $\Delta L = 0.055 \text{ mm} = 0.055 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, the original length is $L_0 = 1 \text{ m}$, and the coefficient of linear expansion is $\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.

Substituting the values into the formula:

$0.055 \times 10^{-3} = 1 \times 11 \times 10^{-6} \times \Delta T$

$\Delta T = \dfrac{0.055 \times 10^{-3}}{11 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{55 \times 10^{-6}}{11 \times 10^{-6}} = 5^\circ C$

The scale gives accurate readings at $20^\circ C$. Therefore, the maximum allowable temperature deviation is $5^\circ C$ above or below this temperature.

The temperature range within which the experiment can be conducted is:

$T = 20^\circ C \pm 5^\circ C$

This gives a range from $15^\circ C$ to $25^\circ C$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $0^\circ C$, the density of water is $0.998 \text{ g cm}^{-3}$, while at $4^\circ C$ it is $1.000 \text{ g cm}^{-3}$. Determine the average coefficient of volume expansion of water over the temperature range of $0$ to $4^\circ C$.
questions.solution:
The relation between volume and temperature is given by:

$V_2 = V_1(1 + \gamma \Delta T)$

Since mass $m$ remains constant, volume is inversely proportional to density ($V = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$). Substituting this into the equation, we get:

$\dfrac{m}{\rho_2} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_1}(1 + \gamma \Delta T)$

$\dfrac{\rho_1}{\rho_2} = 1 + \gamma \Delta T$

Substituting the given values $\rho_1 = 0.998 \text{ g cm}^{-3}$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$, $\rho_2 = 1.000 \text{ g cm}^{-3}$ at $4^\circ\text{C}$, and $\Delta T = 4^\circ\text{C}$:

$\dfrac{0.998}{1.000} = 1 + \gamma (4)$

$0.998 = 1 + 4\gamma$

$4\gamma = 0.998 - 1$

$4\gamma = -0.002$

$\gamma = -0.0005 \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1} = -5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the ratio of the lengths of an iron rod and an aluminium rod such that the difference in their lengths remains independent of temperature. The coefficients of linear expansion for iron and aluminium are $12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ respectively.
questions.solution:
Let $L_1$ and $L_2$ be the lengths of the iron and aluminium rods respectively.

Let $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ be their coefficients of linear expansion.

The change in length for a temperature change $\Delta T$ is given by $\Delta L = L \alpha \Delta T$.

For the difference in lengths to remain independent of temperature, the change in length of both rods must be equal.

$\Delta L_1 = \Delta L_2$

$L_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T = L_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T$

$L_1 \alpha_1 = L_2 \alpha_2$

$\dfrac{L_1}{L_2} = \dfrac{\alpha_2}{\alpha_1}$

Substituting the given values, $\alpha_1 = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $\alpha_2 = 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$:

$\dfrac{L_1}{L_2} = \dfrac{23 \times 10^{-6}}{12 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{23}{12}$

The ratio of their lengths is $23 : 12$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pendulum clock, comprising a light steel rod attached to a heavy ball, indicates the correct time at $20^\circ \text{C}$ in a location where $g = 9.800 \text{ m s}^{-2}$. If the clock is moved to a new location where $g = 9.788 \text{ m s}^{-2}$, at what temperature will it indicate the correct time? The coefficient of linear expansion for steel is $12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The time period of a pendulum is given by $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}$.

For the clock to indicate the correct time at the new location, its time period must remain constant, which implies:

$\dfrac{L_1}{g_1} = \dfrac{L_2}{g_2}$

$\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1}$

Using the formula for linear expansion, $L_2 = L_1(1 + \alpha \Delta \theta)$, where $\Delta \theta = \theta_2 - \theta_1$:

$1 + \alpha \Delta \theta = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1}$

$\alpha \Delta \theta = \dfrac{g_2 - g_1}{g_1}$

Substituting the given values $g_1 = 9.800 \text{ m s}^{-2}$, $g_2 = 9.788 \text{ m s}^{-2}$, and $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$:

$12 \times 10^{-6} \times \Delta \theta = \dfrac{9.788 - 9.800}{9.800}$

$12 \times 10^{-6} \times \Delta \theta = \dfrac{-0.012}{9.800}$

$\Delta \theta = \dfrac{-0.012}{9.800 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}}$

$\Delta \theta = \dfrac{-1000}{9.8} \approx -102^\circ \text{C}$

Since $\Delta \theta = \theta_2 - \theta_1$ and $\theta_1 = 20^\circ \text{C}$:

$\theta_2 - 20 = -102$

$\theta_2 = -82^\circ \text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A constant volume thermometer indicates a pressure of $1.500 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}$ at the triple point of water, while at the normal boiling point it indicates $2.050 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}$. Determine the temperature at the normal boiling point.
questions.solution:
For a constant volume gas thermometer, the temperature $T$ is given by the relation:

$T = T_{tr} \times \dfrac{P}{P_{tr}}$

where $T_{tr} = 273.16 \text{ K}$ is the temperature at the triple point of water.

Given:
$P_{tr} = 1.500 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$
$P = 2.050 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$

Substituting the values:

$T = 273.16 \times \dfrac{2.050 \times 10^4}{1.500 \times 10^4}$

$T = 273.16 \times \dfrac{205}{150}$

$T = 273.16 \times \dfrac{41}{30}$

$T = 373.318 \text{ K}$

Rounding off to one decimal place, we get $373.3 \text{ K}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a constant volume gas thermometer, the pressure of the gas is $70 \text{ kPa}$ at the ice point. Determine the pressure at the steam point.
questions.solution:
For a constant volume gas thermometer, the pressure of the gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

$\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$

Given:
Pressure at ice point, $P_1 = 70 \text{ kPa}$
Absolute temperature at ice point, $T_1 = 273.15 \text{ K}$
Absolute temperature at steam point, $T_2 = 373.15 \text{ K}$

Substituting the values:

$P_2 = 70 \times \dfrac{373.15}{273.15}$

$P_2 \approx 70 \times 1.366$

$P_2 \approx 95.62 \text{ kPa}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the pressure at the steam point is $96 \text{ kPa}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The bulb of a Callender's compensated constant pressure air thermometer has a volume of $1800 \text{ cc}$. When this bulb is immersed in a vessel, $200 \text{ cc}$ of mercury must be poured out. Determine the temperature of the vessel.
questions.solution:
In Callender's compensated constant pressure air thermometer, the main system and the compensating system have equal pressures and equal number of moles of air.

The equation governing the thermometer is given by:

$\dfrac{V}{T} + \dfrac{v_1}{T_0} = \dfrac{V}{T_0} + \dfrac{v_2}{T_0}$

where $V$ is the volume of the bulb, $T$ is the temperature of the vessel, $T_0$ is the temperature of the ice bath ($273 \text{ K}$), and $v_1 - v_2 = v$ is the volume of mercury poured out to maintain constant pressure.

Rearranging the equation, we get:

$\dfrac{V}{T} = \dfrac{V}{T_0} - \dfrac{v_1 - v_2}{T_0}$

$\dfrac{V}{T} = \dfrac{V - v}{T_0}$

$\Rightarrow T = T_0 \left( \dfrac{V}{V - v} \right)$

Given:
$V = 1800 \text{ cc}$
$v = 200 \text{ cc}$
$T_0 = 273 \text{ K}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$T = 273 \times \dfrac{1800}{1800 - 200}$

$T = 273 \times \dfrac{1800}{1600}$

$T = 273 \times \dfrac{9}{8}$

$T = 307.125 \text{ K}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $T \approx 307 \text{ K}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel metre scale measures the correct length at $16^\circ C$. Evaluate the percentage error if this scale is employed on a winter day when the temperature is $6^\circ C$. The coefficient of linear expansion of steel is given as $11 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The true length of an object is $L$.

The scale is calibrated at $T_1 = 16^\circ C$. At $T_2 = 6^\circ C$, the change in temperature is $\Delta T = 6^\circ C - 16^\circ C = -10^\circ C$.

Due to thermal contraction, the length of one division on the scale becomes $d' = d(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$.

The measured length $L_m$ is given by $L_m = \dfrac{L}{1 + \alpha \Delta T} \approx L(1 - \alpha \Delta T)$.

The error in measurement is $\Delta L = L_m - L = -L \alpha \Delta T$.

Percentage error $= \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \times 100 = -\alpha \Delta T \times 100$.

Substituting the given values:

Percentage error $= -(11 \times 10^{-6}) \times (-10) \times 100 = 11 \times 10^{-3} = 0.011\%$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A horizontal aluminium plate has a hole with a diameter of $2.000 \text{ cm}$. A steel sphere having a diameter of $2.005 \text{ cm}$ rests on this hole. These lengths are measured at an initial temperature of $10^\circ \text{C}$. The temperature of the entire system is then gradually increased. Determine the temperature at which the ball will fall through the hole. The coefficient of linear expansion for aluminium is $23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and for steel is $11 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Let the initial temperature be $T_1 = 10^\circ \text{C}$ and the final temperature be $T_2$.

The ball will fall through the hole when the diameter of the hole becomes equal to the diameter of the sphere.

$D_{Al}(1 + \alpha_{Al}\Delta T) = D_{Fe}(1 + \alpha_{Fe}\Delta T)$

$\Delta T = \dfrac{D_{Fe} - D_{Al}}{D_{Al}\alpha_{Al} - D_{Fe}\alpha_{Fe}}$

$\Delta T = \dfrac{2.005 - 2.000}{2.000 \times 23 \times 10^{-6} - 2.005 \times 11 \times 10^{-6}}$

$\Delta T = \dfrac{0.005}{(46 - 22.055) \times 10^{-6}}$

$\Delta T = \dfrac{0.005}{23.945 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 208.8^\circ \text{C}$

$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta T = 10^\circ \text{C} + 208.8^\circ \text{C} = 218.8^\circ \text{C} \approx 219^\circ \text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A glass window is to be installed in an aluminium frame. On the working day, the temperature is $40^\circ C$ and the glass window measures exactly $20 \text{ cm} \times 30 \text{ cm}$. Determine the required size of the aluminium frame so that no stress is exerted on the glass in winter, even if the temperature falls to $0^\circ C$. The coefficients of linear expansion for glass and aluminium are $9.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and $24 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$, respectively.
questions.solution:
Let the dimensions of the glass at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ be $L_{g1} = 20\text{ cm}$ and $L_{g2} = 30\text{ cm}$.

Let the dimensions of the aluminium frame at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ be $L_{a1}$ and $L_{a2}$.

When the temperature falls to $0^{\circ}\text{C}$, the drop in temperature is $\Delta T = 40^{\circ}\text{C}$.

To ensure no stress is exerted on the glass, the dimensions of the aluminium frame at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ must be equal to the dimensions of the glass at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$.

$L_{a1}(1 - \alpha_a \Delta T) = L_{g1}(1 - \alpha_g \Delta T)$

$L_{a1} = L_{g1} \dfrac{1 - \alpha_g \Delta T}{1 - \alpha_a \Delta T} \approx L_{g1} [1 + (\alpha_a - \alpha_g) \Delta T]$

Substituting the given values:

$\alpha_a - \alpha_g = (24 - 9) \times 10^{-6} = 15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$

$(\alpha_a - \alpha_g) \Delta T = 15 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 600 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0006$

$L_{a1} = 20 (1 + 0.0006) = 20.012\text{ cm}$

$L_{a2} = 30 (1 + 0.0006) = 30.018\text{ cm}$

The required size of the aluminium frame is $20.012\text{ cm} \times 30.018\text{ cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A glass vessel has a volume of $1000 \text{ cc}$ at $20^\circ C$. Determine the volume of mercury that must be poured into the vessel at this temperature to ensure that the volume of the remaining space remains constant as the temperature varies. The coefficients of cubical expansion for mercury and glass are $1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and $9.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$, respectively.
questions.solution:
Let $V_g$ be the volume of the glass vessel and $V_m$ be the volume of mercury.

The volume of the empty space is $V_g - V_m$. For this volume to remain constant with temperature, the change in volume of the glass vessel must be equal to the change in volume of the mercury.

$\Delta V_g = \Delta V_m$

$V_g \gamma_g \Delta T = V_m \gamma_m \Delta T$

$V_m = V_g \dfrac{\gamma_g}{\gamma_m}$

Substituting the given values:

$V_m = 1000 \times \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-4}}$

$V_m = 1000 \times \dfrac{9}{180}$

$V_m = 1000 \times \dfrac{1}{20}$

$V_m = 50 \text{ cc}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $0^\circ C$, a glass vessel has exact dimensions of $10 \text{ cm} \times 10 \text{ cm} \times 10 \text{ cm}$. At this temperature, the vessel is completely filled with mercury. Upon raising the temperature to $10^\circ C$, $1.6 \text{ cm}^3$ of mercury overflows. Determine the coefficient of volume expansion of mercury. The coefficient of linear expansion of glass is given as $6.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The initial volume of the glass vessel and mercury is $V_0 = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$.

The change in temperature is $\Delta T = 10^{\circ}\text{C} - 0^{\circ}\text{C} = 10^{\circ}\text{C}$.

The coefficient of volume expansion of glass is $\gamma_g = 3\alpha_g = 3 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-6} = 19.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$.

The volume of mercury that overflows is given by the apparent expansion of mercury:

$\Delta V = V_0 \gamma_{app} \Delta T$

Substituting the given values:

$1.6 = 1000 \times \gamma_{app} \times 10$

$\gamma_{app} = \dfrac{1.6}{10000} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$

The apparent coefficient of volume expansion is related to the absolute coefficient of volume expansion of mercury ($\gamma_{Hg}$) and that of glass ($\gamma_g$) by:

$\gamma_{app} = \gamma_{Hg} - \gamma_g$

$\gamma_{Hg} = \gamma_{app} + \gamma_g$

$\gamma_{Hg} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} + 19.5 \times 10^{-6}$

$\gamma_{Hg} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} + 0.195 \times 10^{-4} = 1.795 \times 10^{-4} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $0^\circ \text{C}$, the densities of wood and benzene are $880 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$ and $900 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, respectively. The coefficients of volume expansion for wood and benzene are $1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, respectively. Determine the temperature at which a piece of wood will just sink in benzene.
questions.solution:
For the piece of wood to just sink in benzene, the density of the wood must become equal to the density of benzene at that temperature $T$.

The density of a substance at temperature $T$ is given by:
$\rho_T = \dfrac{\rho_0}{1 + \gamma T}$

Equating the densities of wood and benzene at temperature $T$:
$\rho_{wT} = \rho_{bT}$

$\dfrac{\rho_{w0}}{1 + \gamma_w T} = \dfrac{\rho_{b0}}{1 + \gamma_b T}$

Cross-multiplying, we get:
$\rho_{w0} (1 + \gamma_b T) = \rho_{b0} (1 + \gamma_w T)$

Substituting the given values:
$880 (1 + 1.5 \times 10^{-3} T) = 900 (1 + 1.2 \times 10^{-3} T)$

$880 + 880 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} T = 900 + 900 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} T$

$880 + 1.32 T = 900 + 1.08 T$

$1.32 T - 1.08 T = 900 - 880$

$0.24 T = 20$

$T = \dfrac{20}{0.24} = \dfrac{2000}{24} = 83.33^\circ\text{C}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the temperature is $83^\circ\text{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Resting on a fixed horizontal base, a steel rod is clamped at both of its ends. At $20^\circ C$, the rod is in an unstrained state. Determine the longitudinal strain developed in the rod when the temperature increases to $50^\circ C$. The coefficient of linear expansion for steel is $1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Given:

Initial temperature, $T_1 = 20^\circ C$

Final temperature, $T_2 = 50^\circ C$

Change in temperature, $\Delta T = 50^\circ C - 20^\circ C = 30^\circ C$

Coefficient of linear expansion, $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$

If the rod were free to expand, the increase in length would be $\Delta L = L \alpha \Delta T$.

Since the rod is clamped at both ends, it cannot expand. The rigid supports exert a compressive force to maintain the original length.

The longitudinal (mechanical) strain developed in the rod is given by:

$\text{Strain} = -\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = -\alpha \Delta T$

Substituting the given values:

$\text{Strain} = -(1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 30$

$\text{Strain} = -3.6 \times 10^{-4}$

The negative sign indicates that the strain developed is compressive.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An iron railway track is installed in winter at an average temperature of $18^\circ\text{C}$. The track is made up of $12.0\text{ m}$ sections placed consecutively. What gap must be provided between two adjacent sections to prevent compression in summer, when the peak temperature reaches $48^\circ\text{C}$? The coefficient of linear expansion for iron is $11 \times 10^{-6}\text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The gap to be provided between two adjacent sections must be equal to the expansion of one section to prevent compression.

The initial length of the track section is $L = 12.0\text{ m}$.

The change in temperature is $\Delta T = 48^\circ\text{C} - 18^\circ\text{C} = 30^\circ\text{C}$.

The coefficient of linear expansion is $\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-6}\text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.

The increase in length of one section is given by:

$\Delta L = L \alpha \Delta T$

Substituting the given values:

$\Delta L = 12.0 \times 11 \times 10^{-6} \times 30$

$\Delta L = 3960 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}$

$\Delta L = 3.96 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}$

Converting to centimeters:

$\Delta L = 0.396\text{ cm}$

Thus, a gap of $0.396\text{ cm}$ must be provided.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel wire with a cross-sectional area of $0.5 \text{ mm}^2$ is secured between two fixed supports. The wire is just taut at $20^\circ \text{C}$. Calculate the tension in the wire when the temperature drops to $0^\circ \text{C}$. The coefficient of linear expansion for steel is $1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$ and its Young's modulus is $2.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N m}^{-2}$.
questions.solution:
The thermal stress developed in the wire due to a change in temperature is given by $\dfrac{F}{A} = Y \alpha \Delta T$

The tension in the wire is $F = Y A \alpha \Delta T$

Given:
$Y = 2.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N m}^{-2}$
$A = 0.5 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
$\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$
$\Delta T = 20^\circ \text{C} - 0^\circ \text{C} = 20^\circ \text{C}$

Substituting the values:
$F = (2.0 \times 10^{11}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 20$

$F = 1.0 \times 10^5 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 20$

$F = 1.2 \times 20 = 24 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel rod is clamped rigidly at both ends. At $20^\circ C$, the rod experiences zero tension. If the temperature increases to $100^\circ C$, determine the force exerted by the rod on one of the clamps. The cross-sectional area of the rod is $2.00 \text{ mm}^2$. The coefficient of linear expansion of steel is $12.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and the Young's modulus of steel is $2.00 \times 10^{11} \text{ N m}^{-2}$.
questions.solution:
The thermal stress developed in a rigidly clamped rod due to a change in temperature is given by $\sigma = Y \alpha \Delta T$.

The force exerted by the rod on the clamps is $F = \sigma A = Y A \alpha \Delta T$.

Substituting the given values $Y = 2.00 \times 10^{11} \text{ N m}^{-2}$, $A = 2.00 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $\alpha = 12.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$, and $\Delta T = 100^{\circ}\text{C} - 20^{\circ}\text{C} = 80^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$F = (2.00 \times 10^{11}) \times (2.00 \times 10^{-6}) \times (12.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times 80$

$F = 4 \times 12 \times 80 \times 10^{-1}$

$F = 384 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An aluminium rod and two steel rods, all having an identical length $l_0$ and equal cross-sectional areas, are rigidly joined at their ends as shown in the figure. At $0^\circ C$, all the rods are in a state of zero tension. Determine the length of the system when its temperature is increased to $\theta$. The coefficients of linear expansion for aluminium and steel are $\alpha_a$ and $\alpha_s$, respectively, while their Young's moduli are $Y_a$ and $Y_s$.
questions.solution:
Let the final length of the composite system be $l$ and the cross-sectional area of each rod be $A$.

The natural length of the aluminium rod at temperature $\theta$ is $l_{0a} = l_0(1 + \alpha_a \theta)$.

The natural length of each steel rod at temperature $\theta$ is $l_{0s} = l_0(1 + \alpha_s \theta)$.

The mechanical strain in the aluminium rod is $\epsilon_a = \dfrac{l - l_{0a}}{l_0}$.

The mechanical strain in each steel rod is $\epsilon_s = \dfrac{l - l_{0s}}{l_0}$.

The tension in the aluminium rod is $F_a = Y_a A \epsilon_a = Y_a A \left(\dfrac{l - l_{0a}}{l_0}\right)$.

The tension in each steel rod is $F_s = Y_s A \epsilon_s = Y_s A \left(\dfrac{l - l_{0s}}{l_0}\right)$.

Since the rods are rigidly joined and there are no external forces, the net internal force must be zero:

$F_a + 2F_s = 0$

$Y_a A \left(\dfrac{l - l_{0a}}{l_0}\right) + 2 Y_s A \left(\dfrac{l - l_{0s}}{l_0}\right) = 0$

$Y_a (l - l_{0a}) + 2 Y_s (l - l_{0s}) = 0$

$l(Y_a + 2Y_s) = Y_a l_{0a} + 2 Y_s l_{0s}$

Substituting the values of $l_{0a}$ and $l_{0s}$:

$l = \dfrac{Y_a l_0(1 + \alpha_a \theta) + 2 Y_s l_0(1 + \alpha_s \theta)}{Y_a + 2Y_s}$

$l = l_0 \dfrac{Y_a + 2Y_s + Y_a \alpha_a \theta + 2 Y_s \alpha_s \theta}{Y_a + 2Y_s}$

$l = l_0 \left[ 1 + \dfrac{\alpha_a Y_a + 2 \alpha_s Y_s}{Y_a + 2Y_s} \theta \right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: While maintaining a constant volume, a steel ball initially at a pressure of $1.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$ is heated from $20^\circ C$ to $120^\circ C$. Determine the pressure inside the ball. The coefficient of linear expansion of steel is $12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and the bulk modulus of steel is $1.6 \times 10^{11} \text{ N m}^{-2}$.
questions.solution:
The fractional change in volume due to a temperature change $\Delta T$ is given by

$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \gamma \Delta T = 3\alpha \Delta T$

To maintain a constant volume, the increase in pressure must counteract this thermal expansion. The required change in pressure $\Delta P$ is related to the bulk modulus $B$ by

$\Delta P = B \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = B (3\alpha) \Delta T$

Given values are:

$B = 1.6 \times 10^{11} \text{ N m}^{-2}$

$\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

$\Delta T = 120^\circ\text{C} - 20^\circ\text{C} = 100^\circ\text{C}$

Substituting these values:

$\Delta P = (1.6 \times 10^{11}) \times (3 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100$

$\Delta P = 1.6 \times 10^{11} \times 36 \times 10^{-4}$

$\Delta P = 57.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 5.76 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}$

The final pressure inside the ball is the sum of the initial pressure and the change in pressure:

$P = P_{initial} + \Delta P$

$P = 1.0 \times 10^{5} + 5.76 \times 10^{8} \approx 5.8 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two metre scales, one of steel and the other of aluminium, have exactly the same readings at $20^\circ C$. Find the ratio aluminium-centimetre/steel-centimetre at $40^\circ C$. Given that $\alpha$ for steel $= 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and for aluminium $= 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Let the true length of $1\text{ cm}$ on both scales at $20^\circ\text{C}$ be $l_0$.

At $40^\circ\text{C}$, the physical length of $1\text{ cm}$ on the aluminium scale (aluminium-centimetre) becomes:

$l_{Al} = l_0(1 + \alpha_{Al}\Delta T)$

Similarly, the physical length of $1\text{ cm}$ on the steel scale (steel-centimetre) becomes:

$l_{St} = l_0(1 + \alpha_{St}\Delta T)$

The ratio of aluminium-centimetre to steel-centimetre at $40^\circ\text{C}$ is given by:

$\dfrac{l_{Al}}{l_{St}} = \dfrac{1 + \alpha_{Al}\Delta T}{1 + \alpha_{St}\Delta T}$

Since $\alpha_{St}\Delta T \ll 1$, using the binomial approximation $(1 + x)^{-1} \approx 1 - x$:

$\dfrac{l_{Al}}{l_{St}} \approx (1 + \alpha_{Al}\Delta T)(1 - \alpha_{St}\Delta T) \approx 1 + (\alpha_{Al} - \alpha_{St})\Delta T$

Given $\alpha_{Al} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}\text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, $\alpha_{St} = 1.1 \times 10^{-5}\text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, and $\Delta T = 40^\circ\text{C} - 20^\circ\text{C} = 20^\circ\text{C}$:

$\dfrac{l_{Al}}{l_{St}} = 1 + (2.3 \times 10^{-5} - 1.1 \times 10^{-5}) \times 20$

$\dfrac{l_{Al}}{l_{St}} = 1 + (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 20$

$\dfrac{l_{Al}}{l_{St}} = 1 + 24 \times 10^{-5} = 1.00024$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An iron circular disc is rotated about its axis with a constant angular velocity $\omega$. Determine the percentage change in the linear speed of a particle on the rim when the disc is gradually heated from $20^\circ C$ to $50^\circ C$ while maintaining the angular velocity constant. The coefficient of linear expansion of iron is $1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The linear speed of a particle on the rim of the disc is given by $v = R \omega$, where $R$ is the radius and $\omega$ is the angular velocity.

Since the angular velocity $\omega$ is maintained constant, the fractional change in the linear speed is equal to the fractional change in the radius.

$\dfrac{\Delta v}{v} = \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$

Due to thermal expansion, the change in radius is given by $\Delta R = R \alpha \Delta T$, which implies $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \alpha \Delta T$.

Therefore, the percentage change in linear speed is:

$\dfrac{\Delta v}{v} \times 100 = \alpha \Delta T \times 100$

Substituting the given values, $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$ and $\Delta T = 50^{\circ}\text{C} - 20^{\circ}\text{C} = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$:

Percentage change $= 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 30 \times 100 = 0.036$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid body of any shape possesses a moment of inertia $I_0$ at $0^\circ C$. If $\alpha$ represents the coefficient of linear expansion of the solid, determine the expression for its moment of inertia $I$ at a temperature $\theta$.
questions.solution:
The moment of inertia of a solid body is given by $I_0 = \sum m_i r_i^2$.

When the temperature of the body increases by $\theta$, the distance of each mass element from the axis of rotation increases due to thermal expansion. The new distance $r_i'$ is given by:

$r_i' = r_i(1 + \alpha \theta)$

where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of linear expansion.

The new moment of inertia $I$ at temperature $\theta$ is:

$I = \sum m_i (r_i')^2$

$I = \sum m_i [r_i(1 + \alpha \theta)]^2$

$I = (1 + \alpha \theta)^2 \sum m_i r_i^2$

$I = I_0 (1 + \alpha \theta)^2$

Since $\alpha$ is very small, we can use the binomial approximation $(1 + x)^n \approx 1 + nx$ for $x \ll 1$ and neglect higher-order terms of $\alpha$:

$I = I_0(1 + 2\alpha \theta)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical aluminium can holds $500 \text{ cm}^3$ of water, with the can's inner cross-sectional area being $125 \text{ cm}^2$. All these measurements are taken at $10^\circ \text{C}$. Determine the rise in the water level when the temperature is raised to $80^\circ \text{C}$. The coefficient of linear expansion of aluminium is $23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and the average coefficient of volume expansion of water is $3.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Initial volume of water, $V_{0} = 500 \text{ cm}^{3}$

Initial area of cross-section of the can, $A_{0} = 125 \text{ cm}^{2}$

Initial height of the water level, $h_{0} = \dfrac{V_{0}}{A_{0}} = \dfrac{500}{125} = 4 \text{ cm}$

Change in temperature, $\Delta T = 80^{\circ}\text{C} - 10^{\circ}\text{C} = 70^{\circ}\text{C}$

After the temperature is raised, the new volume of water is:
$V = V_{0}(1 + \gamma \Delta T)$

The new inner cross-sectional area of the aluminium can is:
$A = A_{0}(1 + 2\alpha \Delta T)$

The new height of the water level is:
$h = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{V_{0}(1 + \gamma \Delta T)}{A_{0}(1 + 2\alpha \Delta T)} = h_{0}\dfrac{1 + \gamma \Delta T}{1 + 2\alpha \Delta T}$

Since $2\alpha \Delta T \ll 1$, using binomial approximation:
$h \approx h_{0}(1 + \gamma \Delta T)(1 - 2\alpha \Delta T) \approx h_{0}(1 + (\gamma - 2\alpha)\Delta T)$

The rise in the water level is:
$\Delta h = h - h_{0} = h_{0}(\gamma - 2\alpha)\Delta T$

Given:
$\gamma = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1} = 320 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$
$\alpha = 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$

Substituting the values:
$\gamma - 2\alpha = 320 \times 10^{-6} - 2(23 \times 10^{-6}) = 274 \times 10^{-6} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$

$\Delta h = 4 \times (274 \times 10^{-6}) \times 70$

$\Delta h = 280 \times 274 \times 10^{-6} = 76720 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}$

$\Delta h = 0.07672 \text{ cm} \approx 0.076 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel rod of length $1 \text{ m}$ is placed on a smooth horizontal base. When it is heated from $0^\circ \text{C}$ to $100^\circ \text{C}$, what is the longitudinal strain developed?
questions.solution:
When a rod is heated, it undergoes thermal expansion.

If the rod is free to expand, such as when it is placed on a smooth horizontal base, no internal restoring forces are generated within the material. Thermal stress is developed only when the natural expansion of the rod is restricted by external forces or supports.

Since there is no restriction on the expansion of the steel rod in this case, the thermal stress developed is zero.

According to Hooke's law, the mechanical strain developed is directly proportional to the stress. Because the stress is zero, the longitudinal strain developed in the rod is also zero. (Note that the actual change in length is not considered as mechanical strain unless it is caused by a deforming force).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A torsional pendulum comprises a solid disc attached at its centre to a thin wire ($\alpha = 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$). Determine the percentage change in the time period between peak winter ($5^\circ\text{C}$) and peak summer ($45^\circ\text{C}$).
questions.solution:
The time period of a torsional pendulum is given by:

$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{C}}$

where $I$ is the moment of inertia of the disc and $C$ is the torsional constant of the wire.

The torsional constant of a cylindrical wire is given by:

$C = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L}$

where $\eta$ is the shear modulus, $r$ is the radius, and $L$ is the length of the wire. When the temperature increases by $\Delta T$, the dimensions of the wire change due to thermal expansion. The new radius and length become:

$r' = r(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$
$L' = L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$

The new torsional constant $C'$ is:

$C' = \dfrac{\pi \eta (r')^4}{2L'} = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^4 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)^4}{2L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)} \approx C(1 + 3\alpha \Delta T)$

Since the coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ is given only for the thin wire, we assume the moment of inertia $I$ of the disc remains constant. The new time period $T'$ is:

$T' = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{C(1 + 3\alpha \Delta T)}} = T(1 + 3\alpha \Delta T)^{-1/2}$

Using the binomial approximation for small $\alpha \Delta T$:

$T' \approx T\left(1 - \dfrac{3}{2}\alpha \Delta T\right)$

The magnitude of the fractional change in the time period is:

$\left| \dfrac{\Delta T_{\text{period}}}{T} \right| = \dfrac{3}{2}\alpha \Delta T$

Given values:
$\alpha = 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
$\Delta T = 45^\circ\text{C} - 5^\circ\text{C} = 40^\circ\text{C}$

Substituting the values to find the percentage change:

$\text{Percentage change} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times (2.4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 40 \times 100\%$

$\text{Percentage change} = 1.5 \times 9.6 \times 10^{-4} \times 100\% = 0.144\%$

Answer: $0.144\%$
